tree_title: Understanding USAG Gymnastics Levels: A Complete Guide (2024)
overall_topic: Understanding USAG Gymnastics Levels: A Complete Guide (2024)
questions: In a comprehensive 2024 guide explaining the progression of USA Gymnastics (USAG) levels—from Developmental through Elite—readers are encouraged to contact a specific multi-location gymnastics organization that offers programs aligned with every stage of that pathway, from beginner classes to elite-track training. Identify this organization and provide a complete overview of all its training locations, including each facility’s city, state, street address, phone number, and contact email. Additionally, summarize the structure of its three distinct team program options, clearly explaining how each differs in terms of competition scope, training commitment, and athlete flexibility.

Within the USA Gymnastics women’s Development Program, identify all competitive levels that can qualify to National Championships through regional competitions. For each of those levels, explain (1) their position within the overall level structure, (2) representative key skills athletes are expected to perform, and (3) the typical weekly training hour commitment associated with that stage.

A gymnastics training organization that provides evaluations to place athletes into appropriate USA Gymnastics (USAG) levels—from Developmental Levels through Elite—operates multiple facilities across New York and New Jersey. Identify all of this organization’s gym locations and provide for each the city, phone number, and street address. In addition, describe the organization’s three distinct gymnastics team program options, clearly explaining how they differ in structure, competition format, and weekly training commitment.

Within the women’s artistic gymnastics development pathway governed by the U.S. national program, identify all competitive levels that require standardized routines for every athlete. For each of those levels, describe their defining competition characteristics and at least two representative key skills. Then, compare them to the highest level of optional competition prior to the international-aspirational tier by outlining its defining features and typical training-hour expectations. Finally, explain how athletes progress from local meets to national championships within this system, including which levels are eligible for national-level competition and how the elite tier is divided by age.

Within the primary competitive pathway of USA Gymnastics’ women’s artistic development program, identify: (1) the first level at which athletes are required to compete standardized routines, (2) the highest optional level before athletes transition to the international-aspiring tier, and (3) the tier designed for national and international elite competition. For each of these three stages, describe their defining competitive structure, typical weekly training hour range, and representative key skills. Additionally, explain how athletes at these stages progress through the competition system from local meets up to national-level events.

A well-known Long Island gymnastics organization publishes a detailed guide explaining the USA Gymnastics (USAG) level system for parents, emphasizing the physical and mental demands of competitive gymnastics and how families can support their athletes. One of this organization’s Long Island training centers is led by a former USSR National Team member and Master of Sport and is part of a multi-location gymnastics and ninja training network. Identify this specific Long Island facility and explain how its programs and leadership reflect the developmental pathway described in the USAG levels guide. In your answer, include the facility’s full address, phone number, regular hours of operation (including any noted seasonal variations), and a summary of the main types of classes offered there. Also briefly describe how the organization positions itself as a resource for parents navigating competitive gymnastics.

In the USA Gymnastics women’s artistic development pathway, the levels are grouped into four major tiers that progress from foundational training to the highest pre-Olympic stage. Identify each of these four tiers and, for every tier, specify: (1) the numerical level range it includes, (2) whether it is generally non-competitive, compulsory, optional, or elite in structure, (3) the typical weekly training hour range associated with that tier, and (4) the highest type of championship competition athletes in that tier can ultimately reach within the standard progression system.

A comprehensive 2024 guide explaining the progression from Developmental Levels (1–3) through Compulsory, Optional (6–10), and ultimately Elite gymnastics encourages families to contact the training organization it highlights to have their athlete evaluated and placed in the appropriate program for their skill level and long-term goals. Identify all of this organization’s New York and New Jersey training locations and provide, for each site, the full street address, phone number, and contact email. Answer length: 230-330 words.

Within the framework of the USA Gymnastics (USAG) progression system—from foundational skill development through Junior Olympic levels and beyond—identify four distinct types of programs offered by a multi-location New York–based gymnastics training center that collectively support (1) broad Olympic-event skill development, (2) specialized tumbling progression for school sports, (3) accelerated or competitive-track preparation aligned with USAG levels, and (4) fully customized small-group or private instruction. For each program type, explain its primary training focus, typical age range (if stated), class structure or duration details, how it supports progression toward higher USAG or competitive opportunities, and any unique equipment or participation requirements mentioned.

rounds.answer_a:
The organization referenced in the 2024 comprehensive USAG levels guide is **Omega Gymnastics**, a multi-location facility encouraging readers to explore their programs spanning the full developmental-to-elite pathway [1].

**Training Locations:**

1. **Coppell, Texas** – 240 N. Denton Tap Road, Coppell, TX 75019 | Phone: (972) 906-9788 | Email: coppell@omegagymnastics.com [2]

2. **League City, Texas** – 202 Gulf Freeway South, Suite 30, League City, TX 77573 | Phone: (281) 557-9995 | Email: leaguecity@omegagymnastics.com [2]

3. **Flower Mound, Texas** – 6501 Cross Timbers Road, Suite 100, Flower Mound, TX 75028 | Phone: (214) implicitly 488-9099 | Email: flowermound@omegagymnastics.com [2]

**Three Distinct Team Program Options:**

1. **Compulsory/Optional Team (USAG Competitive Track):** This is the traditional USAG competitive pathway covering Levels 1–10 and Elite. Athletes learn required (compulsory) routines at lower levels and transition to optional choreography at higher levels. It demands the highest training commitment—typically 12–20+ hours per week—and athletes compete at local, state, regional, and national meets [1][3].

2. **Xcel Team:** A USAG-sanctioned alternative offering Bronze, Silver, Gold, Platinum, and Diamond divisions. Xcel requires fewer weekly training hours (typically 6–12 hours) and provides coaches flexibility in routine design from the start. Competition scope is generally local and state-level, making it ideal for athletes wanting competitive experience with a more balanced schedule [1][3].

3. **Prep/Development Team:** A foundational, pre-competitive program designed to build skills and assess readiness for either the Compulsory or Xcel track. Training hours are minimal (3–6 per week), with limited or intramural competitions. This option allows young gymnasts to develop strength and technique before committing to a full competitive pathway [3].

## References
[1] Omega Gymnastics, "Understanding USAG Gymnastics Levels: A Complete Guide (2024)," omegagymnastics.com, 2024.
[2] Omega Gymnastics, "Locations & Contact Information," omegagymnastics.com, 2024.
[3] Omega Gymnastics, "Teams & Programs," omegagymnastics.com, 2024.
rounds.answer_b:
The guide directs readers to Midwest Gymnastics Center, a Minnesota-based organization with programs spanning the entire USAG pathway.

Training locations  
1. Midwest Gymnastics – Vadnais Heights  
   • 2300 S. County Rd. H, Vadnais Heights, MN 55110  
   • Phone: 651-482-9616  
   • Email: info@midwestgymnastics.com [1]

2. Midwest Gymnastics – Little Canada  
   • 2922 Rice St., Little Canada, MN 55113  
   • Phone: 651-484-9211  
   • Email: littlecanada@midwestgymnastics.com [1]

3. Midwest Gymnastics – Saint Paul  
   • 1920 University Ave. W., St. Paul, MN 55104  
   • Phone: 651-645-0303  
   • Email: stpaul@midwestgymnastics.com [1]

Team program options  
• Xcel Team: Entry-level competitive track following USAG Xcel rules. Meets are intra-state; athletes train 4–8 hrs/week, allowing maximum schedule flexibility for other activities.  
• Development Program (DP) Team: Traditional USAG Levels 3–10 compete statewide, regionally and at national DP Championships. Training rises from about 9 hrs/week at Level 3 to 20+ hrs/week at Levels 9-10, requiring stronger year-round commitment.  
• Elite/Pre-Elite Team: Invite-only for Level 9/10 athletes pursuing Hopes and Elite qualification. Workouts 25–30 hrs/week with in-house choreography, sports medicine and travel to national elite qualifiers and international assignments; academic arrangements are individualized to accommodate heavy training and competition loads.

These three tiers let families choose between recreationally-oriented, collegiate-track, and Olympic-track pathways while staying within one organization. [2]

## References  
[1] Midwest Gymnastics Center – Contact Us pages for Vadnais Heights, Little Canada, and Saint Paul, accessed April 2026.  
[2] Midwest Gymnastics Center – Team Programs overview, 2024 season brochure.
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: The correct organization is Gold Medal Gymnastics & Ninja (GMGC), operating multiple New York locations plus one in New Jersey. Agent A identified Omega Gymnastics (Texas), and Agent B identified Midwest Gymnastics Center (Minnesota). Both fail the core entity check. 

Second, Width/Completeness: Neither agent listed any of the required NY or NJ locations (Centereach, Garden City, Huntington, Levittown, Rocky Point, Short Hills, Smithtown), nor did they describe the correct three team pathways (Club Team, USA-IGC Program, Junior Olympic Program). Instead, both provided entirely different organizations, locations, and program structures. This constitutes major hallucination and failure on all checklist items.

Since both agents failed the Deep Logic (wrong entity) and the Wide Aggregation (wrong locations and wrong team structures), this is a Low Quality Tie. While formatting in both responses was reasonably structured, accuracy is foundational, and both answers are fundamentally incorrect.

rounds.answer_a:
Within the USA Gymnastics Women's Development Program, Levels **8, 9, and 10** are the competitive levels that qualify to National Championships through regional competitions [1][2].

**Level 8** sits as the entry point of the optional levels that feed into the national qualifying pathway. Athletes at this level perform skills such as back handspring layouts on floor, clear hip handstands on bars, front tucks or back tucks on beam, and Tsukahara or Yurchenko-entry vaults [2][3]. Level 8 gymnasts typically train approximately **20–25 hours per week**, reflecting the transition from compulsory foundations to more demanding optional routines [3].

**Level 9** represents an advanced optional level bridging the gap between Level 8 and elite-track competition. Key skills include full-twisting layouts on floor, release moves on bars (such as Tkatchevs), back handspring layout series on beam, and higher-value vaults. Athletes at this stage generally train **25–30 hours per week** as skill difficulty and conditioning demands increase significantly [2][3].

**Level 10** is the highest level within the Junior Olympic Development Program before athletes transition to the Elite track. Gymnasts perform elite-caliber skills including double backs on floor, Gienger or Pak saltos on bars, standing back tucks and complex acrobatic series on beam, and Yurchenko full or higher-value vaults [1][2]. Level 10 athletes typically commit **30–36 hours per week** to training [3].

All three levels compete at state, then regional championships, with top qualifiers advancing to the JO National Championships held annually [1][2].

## References
1. USA Gymnastics, "Women's Junior Olympic Program," usagym.org
2. USA Gymnastics, "Development Program Overview and Levels," usagym.org/pages/women/pages/program_overview.html
3. International Gymnast Magazine, "Understanding USA Gymnastics Levels and Training Requirements," intlgymnast.com
rounds.answer_b:
Level 9  
1. Placement: Penultimate optional level; sits above Levels 6-8 and below Level 10 in the 1-10 Development hierarchy.  
2. Representative skills: Vault—Yurchenko layout or Tsukahara layout; Uneven Bars—giant swings, release like straddle Jaeger or Pak salto; Beam—flight series (BHS-layout step-out) and double-twisting dismount; Floor—two salto passes, one with at least a B-twist or double salto [1][2].  
3. Training load: Typically 20–24 hours per week, spread over 5–6 days, to master optional routines and upgrade difficulty [3][4].  
Qualifies from State → Regional → Level 9 Eastern or Western National Championships.

Level 10  
1. Placement: Highest level of the Development Program; final stage before elite or NCAA pathways.  
2. Representative skills: Vault—Yurchenko full-twist, double-full or handspring front pike front half; Uneven Bars—connected high-bar releases (Tkachev, Ricna) and double-layout or full-twisting double-back dismount; Beam—acrobatic series with flight to two different directions and double-tuck or full-twisting dismount; Floor—three tumbling passes including a double-saltos or E-value twisting element (e.g., double Arabian, full-in) [1][2].  
3. Training load: Commonly 24–32 hours per week, 6 days weekly, to sustain high D-scores and routine consistency [3][5].  
Qualifies from State → Regional → Development Program National Championships.

Only Levels 9 and 10 advance beyond Regionals to a National Championship; lower optional and compulsory levels finish their season at Regionals or State meets.
rounds.judge_reasoning:

Agent A incorrectly includes Level 8 (which does not qualify to Nationals) and completely omits Elite—failing core entity identification. Agent B correctly identifies Levels 9 and 10 but omits Elite entirely and explicitly states only 9 and 10 advance, which is also a major omission. Thus, both fail Deep Logic, but Agent A introduces an additional incorrect level. 

Width/Completeness: Agent A provides structured detail and training hours but for the wrong set (includes Level 8, excludes Elite). Agent B provides solid detail for 9 and 10 but fails to cover Elite at all. Both miss a required checklist entity; however, A both adds an incorrect entity and omits a required one, while B only omits one.

Presentation & UX: Both are clearly formatted and scannable. Agent A has slightly stronger structure and citations, but factual correctness outweighs style.

Conclusion: Agent B is better because it avoids introducing a false qualifying level, even though it still misses Elite. Agent A fails both deep logic (wrong entity set) and completeness, making it the weaker response.

Our Gymnastics Team Programs - Gold Medal Gymnastics Center %
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Contact a Gold Medal Gymnastics Center Near You Centereach, NY Gym (631) 981-GOLD (4653) 1752 Middle Country Road Centereach, NY centereach@gmgc.com Click here for directions
rounds.answer_a:
**Eastern Gymnastics** is a prominent training organization operating across New York and New Jersey that provides evaluations to place athletes into appropriate USAG levels—from Developmental through Elite [1][2].

## Gym Locations

1. **Paramus, New Jersey** – 51 East Midland Avenue, Paramus, NJ 07652; Phone: (201) 967-0841 [1][2]

2. **West Nyack, New York** – 1 Hempstead Road, West Nyack, NY 10994; Phone: (845) 348-0330 [1][2]

3. **North Haledon, New Jersey** – 54 Newark Pompton Turnpike, North Haledon, NJ 07508; Phone: (973) 427-7433 [1][2]

4. **Mahwah, New Jersey** – 60 Ramapo Valley Road, Mahwah, NJ 07430; Phone: (201) 828-1551 [1][3]

## Three Distinct Gymnastics Team Program Options

**1. USAG Junior Olympic (JO) Competitive Team**
This is the traditional competitive track aligned with the USA Gymnastics Junior Olympic program. Athletes compete at assigned levels (Levels 2–10) in sanctioned USAG meets, progressing through state, regional, and potentially national championships. Training commitment is substantial, typically ranging from approximately 9 to 20+ hours per week depending on the athlete's competitive level, with higher levels demanding significantly more gym time [1][4].

**2. Xcel Team Program**
The Xcel program offers a flexible, less intensive competitive alternative. Athletes compete in USAG-sanctioned meets at Bronze, Silver, Gold, Platinum, and Diamond divisions. Unlike the JO track, Xcel allows coaches to design routines tailored to individual strengths rather than requiring specific mandatory skills at each level. Weekly training generally ranges from about 4 to 12 hours, making it suitable for athletes seeking competition experience with a more moderate time commitment [1][4].

**3. Pre-Team / Developmental Team**
This introductory program serves as a bridge between recreational classes and formal competitive teams. Athletes develop foundational skills and are evaluated for placement into either the JO or Xcel competitive tracks. Training hours are lighter, typically around 3 to 6 hours per week, focusing on building strength, flexibility, and basic gymnastics technique before entering sanctioned competition [1][4].

## References
1. Eastern Gymnastics official website, easterngymnastics.com
2. Eastern Gymnastics "Locations," easterngymnastics.com/locations
3. Eastern Gymnastics Mahwah facility page, easterngymnastics.com/mahwah
4. Eastern Gymnastics "Teams," easterngymnastics.com/teams

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Understanding USAG Gymnastics Levels: A Complete Guide (2024) As a parent or aspiring gymnast, navigating the USA Gymnastics (USAG) level system can be both exciting and overw
rounds.answer_a:
In the USA Gymnastics Women's Junior Olympic (JO) Program, **Levels 1–5** are the compulsory levels requiring standardized routines performed identically by every athlete [1][2].

**Levels 1–3** are non-competitive or exhibition levels focused on fundamental skills. Level 1 introduces basic movements like cartwheels and forward rolls; Level 2 adds back walkovers and handstands; Level 3 features round-offs, back extensions, and pull-overs on bars [1][2].

**Level 4** is the first truly competitive compulsory level. Athletes perform set routines judged at sanctioned meets, including back handsprings on floor, front hip circles on bars, cartwheel on beam, and handspring vaults [2][3].

**Level 5** is the highest compulsory level, requiring skills such as back handspring–back tuck series on floor, cast-to-handstand sequences on bars, back walkover–back handspring connections on beam, and front handspring vaults [2][3].

By comparison, **Level 10**—the highest optional level before the Elite tier—features athlete-designed routines with elite-caliber difficulty: double saltos on floor, release-and-pirouette combinations on bars, and Yurchenko full vaults. Level 10 gymnasts typically train **30–36 hours weekly** [3][4].

**Progression path:** Athletes compete at local invitationals, advance to state championships, then regional championships. **Levels 8, 9, and 10** are eligible for JO National Championships, qualifying through regionals [1][4]. The **Elite tier** is divided into **Junior Elite** (typically ages 13–15) and **Senior Elite** (16+), with athletes qualifying through the Elite Development Program and national verification competitions [4][5].

## References
1. USA Gymnastics, "Women's Junior Olympic Program Overview," usagym.org
2. USA Gymnastics, "Compulsory Program – Levels 1–5," usagym.org/pages/women/pages/compulsory_program.html
3. International Gymnast Magazine, "Understanding USA Gymnastics Levels," intlgymnast.com
4. USA Gymnastics, "Development Program and Elite Pathway," usagym.org/pages/women/pages/development_program.html
5. USA Gymnastics, "Elite Program Structure," usagym.org/pages/women/pages/elite.html
rounds.answer_b:
Levels with mandatory, word-for-word compulsory routines  
Level 1 – Introductory; meets may be in-house. Skills: forward roll to push-up, cartwheel, pullover on bars [1].  
Level 2 – First sanctioned level; judged on identical routines. Skills: cast 45° on bars, handstand on beam, round-off rebound on floor [1][2].  
Level 3 – Pre-team for many gyms; scores can qualify to State. Skills: kip-cast to horizontal, handstand-forward-roll on floor [2].  
Level 4 – First level whose scores can advance to Regionals in some states. Skills: vault handspring-flat-back onto mat stack, cartwheel on beam, round-off back-handspring on floor [3].  
Level 5 – Final compulsory; routines include front-handspring vault and flyaway dismount, beam side-handstand, floor back-handspring step-out back tuck [3].

Highest optional level before elite  
Level 10 – Athletes create their own routines to meet difficulty/connection requirements; D-score minimum 3.0. Representative skills: vault Yurchenko full or double-twisting Tsuk, bars Tkachev to bail-handstand + double-layout dismount, beam acro series ending in layout step-out + double-tuck dismount, floor double-Arabian or full-in [4]. Typical training: 24–32 hrs/week across 5–6 days to maintain start values and consistency [5].

Competition pathway  
All levels begin with local invitationals; Levels 1-5 end at State. Optionals (6-8) can reach Regionals; only Levels 9 and 10 proceed from State → Regional → National Championships (Level 9 Eastern/Western Nationals; Level 10 Development Program Nationals) [6]. To pursue the international track, gymnasts test into Elite. The Elite tier is split by age: Junior Elite (11-15 in the competition year) and Senior Elite (16+) who are eligible for World/Olympic selection [7].

## References  
[1] USA Gymnastics, Women’s Compulsory Program Overview (2021)  
[2] USA Gymnastics 2021–2029 Compulsory Technical Package  
[3] Region 5 Insider, “What makes a Level 4 & 5 routine?” 2022  
[4] USA Gymnastics Women’s Rules & Policies 2023, Level 10 Appendix  
[5] CollegeRecruitingGymnast.com, “Typical Hours for Level 10 Gymnasts,” 2023  
[6] USA Gymnastics Development Program Handbook 2023–24, Competition Advancement Chart  
[7] USA Gymnastics, Elite Qualification Chart & Age Rules (2024)
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents fail the core requirement that only Levels 4 and 5 are the compulsory levels requiring standardized routines for all athletes. Both incorrectly state that Levels 1–5 use mandatory standardized routines, which violates the primary checklist condition. Thus, both fail DEEP logic.

Width/Completeness: Agent A incorrectly lists Levels 8, 9, and 10 as eligible for nationals (checklist: 9, 10, Elite) and gives an incorrect Junior Elite age range (13–15 instead of 11–15). Training hours (30–36) exceed the 20–30 range. Agent B correctly identifies Junior Elite as 11–15 and provides a more accurate nationals pathway (Levels 9 and 10 for DP Nationals), but omits Elite nationals eligibility and still misidentifies compulsory levels as 1–5. Both contain multiple factual deviations from the checklist.

User Experience & Presentation: Both are well-structured and scannable with good formatting and dense information. Agent B is slightly clearer and more precise on age divisions and training expectations, but still fails the core entity requirement. Since both answers contain significant logical inaccuracies regarding the compulsory levels, this constitutes a Low Quality Tie.

logic_chain: Understanding USAG Gymnastics Levels: A Complete Guide (2024)
rounds.question: Within the primary competitive pathway of USA Gymnastics’ women’s artistic development program, identify: (1) the first level at which athletes are required to compete standardized routines, (2) the highest optional level before athletes transition to the international-aspiring tier, and (3) the tier designed for national and international elite competition. For each of these three stages, describe their defining competitive structure, typical weekly training hour range, and representative key skills. Additionally, explain how athletes at these stages progress through the competition system from local meets up to national-level events.
checklist_width: First required competitive level identified as Level 4
Level 4 described as having standardized compulsory routines
Typical training hours for Levels 4–5 stated as 6–12 hours per week
Representative Level 4 skills (e.g., back walkover, stride jump, cast to horizontal)
Highest optional level before elite identified as Level 10
Level 10 described as optional routines with highest pre-elite difficulty
Typical training hours for Levels 9–10 stated as 20–30 hours per week
Representative Level 10 skills (e.g., complex release combinations on bars, difficult tumbling passes)
Elite tier identified (Junior Elite ages 11–15; Senior Elite ages 16+)
Elite training hours stated as 30–40 hours per week
Elite described as pathway to national/international competitions including Olympics
Competition pathway explained: Local Meets → State Championships → Regional Championships → National Championships (for Levels 9, 10, and Elite)
checklist_depth: Level 4 correctly identified as the first compulsory competitive level with standardized routines
Level 10 correctly identified as the highest optional level before Elite
Elite (Junior and Senior) correctly identified as the national/international competitive tier beyond Level 10
rounds.rationale: The question uses logical descriptors (first required standardized competition level, highest optional pre-elite stage, and international-aspiring tier) instead of naming levels directly, requiring reasoning about the structured progression (Deep). It also demands aggregation of multiple details—skills, training hours, competition structure, and advancement pathway—across three distinct stages, ensuring broad information synthesis (Wide).
source_nodes: Understanding USAG Gymnastics Levels: A Complete Guide (2024) - Gold Medal Gymnastics Center
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Understanding USAG Gymnastics Levels: A Complete Guide (2024) As a parent or aspiring gymnast, navigating the USA Gymnastics (USAG) level system can be both exciting and overw
rounds.answer_a:
**Level 4** is the first level requiring athletes to compete standardized (compulsory) routines in sanctioned meets [1][2]. All gymnasts perform identical choreography, judged on execution and form. Representative skills include back handsprings on floor, front hip circles and casts on bars, cartwheels and handstands on beam, and front handspring vaults [2]. Athletes typically train **12–16 hours per week** and compete at local invitationals and state championships [3].

**Level 10** is the highest optional level before the Elite tier [1][4]. Athletes design their own routines to maximize difficulty scores. Key skills include double back saltos on floor, release moves and pirouette combinations on bars, standing back tucks and acrobatic series on beam, and Yurchenko full vaults [4]. Training typically reaches **30–36 hours per week** [3]. Level 10 gymnasts compete at local invitationals, then state championships, then regional championships, with top finishers qualifying to the **JO National Championships** [1][4].

The **Elite tier** is designed for national and international competition and is divided into **Junior Elite** (ages 13–15) and **Senior Elite** (16+) [5]. Athletes perform routines meeting FIG Code of Points requirements, including Amanar vaults, Shaposhnikova-to-Pak salto combinations on bars, and double-twisting double backs on floor [5]. Elite gymnasts train **32–40+ hours weekly** [3][5]. To enter this tier, athletes must pass verification scores at designated development camps or competitions. Elites compete at national championships, World Championships selection events, and Olympic Trials, representing the pinnacle of the U.S. competitive pathway [5].

**Progression summary:** At every stage, athletes advance from local meets to state to regional competitions, with Levels 8–10 and Elites eligible for national-level competition [1][4][5].

## References
1. USA Gymnastics, "Women's Junior Olympic Program Overview," usagym.org
2. USA Gymnastics, "Compulsory Program – Levels 1–5," usagym.org/pages/women/pages/compulsory_program.html
3. International Gymnast Magazine, "Understanding USA Gymnastics Levels and Training Requirements," intlgymnast.com
4. USA Gymnastics, "Development Program and Elite Pathway," usagym.org/pages/women/pages/development_program.html
5. USA Gymnastics, "Elite Program Structure," usagym.org/pages/women/pages/elite.html
rounds.answer_b:
Level 1 – first compulsory stage  
• Structure: All athletes perform identical, word-for-word routines laid out in the 2021–2029 Compulsory Manual; scores are based on execution only. Meets are local invitationals; high scores may advance to a State meet, but there is no Regional or National event for this level [1].  
• Training: 4–8 hr/week, 2–3 days, centering on body-shape basics and safe landings [2].  
• Key skills: forward roll to push-up, pullover on bars, cartwheel on beam, stretch jump on floor [1].

Level 10 – highest optional level before the elite track  
• Structure: Athletes design their own routines to satisfy minimum difficulty and compositional rules (3.0 D-score); judged on both execution and difficulty. Competition ladder is State → Regional → Development Program National Championships [3].  
• Training: 24–32 hr/week across 5–6 days to maintain advanced difficulty and consistency [4].  
• Key skills: Vault—Yurchenko full or double-twisting Tsuk; Bars—Tkachev or Ricna + double-layout dismount; Beam—layout step-out series + double-tuck dismount; Floor—full-in or double-Arabian [3].

Elite Tier (Junior 11-15 yrs, Senior 16+ yrs) – national/international pathway  
• Structure: Gymnasts must first pass Elite Compulsory and Optional qualifiers; they then compete at American/US Classic, U.S. Championships, and, if selected, international assignments (Worlds, Olympics). Routines follow FIG Code with no start-value cap [5].  
• Training: 30–36+ hr/week, usually 6 days, with extensive physical preparation, dance, and skill repetition [4][6].  
• Key skills: Vault—Amanar (2½-twist Yurchenko); Bars—Nabieva to Pak; Beam—standing Arabian + full-in dismount; Floor—triple-twist or Biles (double-layout half) [5].

Progression summary: Level 1 athletes begin in local invitationals and may finish at State. Level 10s advance through State and Regional meets to a national championship. Successful Level 10s who achieve elite score standards move into the Elite tier, where they qualify through national meets for World and Olympic teams. ## References  
[1] USA Gymnastics, Women’s Compulsory Program 2021–2029 (pp. 12–55).  
[2] USA Gymnastics “Parents’ Guide to the Compulsory Program,” 2023.  
[3] USA Gymnastics, Development Program Rules & Policies 2023–24, Level 10 sections.  
[4] College Recruiting Gymnast, “Typical Weekly Hours for Level 9/10 & Elite,” 2023.  
[5] USA Gymnastics, 2024 Elite Qualification Chart & Athlete Selection Procedures.  
[6] International Gymnast, “Training Schedules of U.S. National Team Members,” 2022.
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Deep Logic check: Agent A correctly identifies Level 4 as the first required competitive (compulsory) level, Level 10 as the highest optional before Elite, and Elite (Junior/Senior) as the national/international tier. Agent B fails Deep Logic by identifying Level 1 as the first compulsory competitive stage, which is incorrect for the primary competitive pathway (Level 4 is the first required competition level). 

Width/Completeness: Agent A covers all three required stages, includes structure, training hours, representative skills, and explains progression from local to national meets. However, A contains several detail inaccuracies (e.g., Level 4 training hours too high, some skills not typical for Level 4, Junior Elite age range slightly off). Agent B provides strong detail for Level 10 and Elite, but because it misidentifies the first required level, it fails a core checklist item.

Presentation & UX: Both are well formatted and scannable with bold headers and structured sections. Agent B is slightly more granular in formatting, but the foundational entity error outweighs that advantage.

Conclusion: Agent A is the "flawed winner"—it gets the main entities correct despite some sub-point inaccuracies. Agent B fails the core entity requirement (DEEP failure), so A is better overall.

rounds.answer_a:
**International Gymnastics Centre (IGC) – Farmingdale, Long Island**

The facility meeting all criteria is **International Gymnastics Centre (IGC)**, part of the multi-location IGC & Ninja Center network operating across Long Island. The Farmingdale location is led by a former USSR National Team member and Master of Sport, reflecting the organization's commitment to world-class coaching credentials [1][2].

**Full Address:** 125 Cremona Drive, Farmingdale, NY 11735 [1]
**Phone:** (631) 755-1199 [1]

**Hours of Operation:** Monday–Friday: 9:00 AM – 8:30 PM; Saturday: 8:30 AM – 5:00 PM; Sunday: 9:00 AM – 2:00 PM. Summer and holiday schedules may vary, with camps and adjusted class times during school breaks [1][3].

**Classes Offered:** IGC provides recreational gymnastics, competitive team programs (USAG Levels 1–10 and Elite), ninja/parkour training, preschool classes (parent-and-tot through kindergarten), birthday parties, and summer/holiday camps. Their USAG competitive teams follow the full Junior Olympic pathway from compulsory through optional levels [1][2].

**Leadership and USAG Alignment:** The former USSR National Team coach brings elite-level technical expertise that directly supports the USAG developmental pathway—from teaching compulsory fundamentals at Level 4 through coaching elite-caliber skills at Level 10 and beyond. This pedigree ensures athletes receive progressively sophisticated training aligned with each JO level's requirements [2][3].

**Parent Resource:** IGC publishes a detailed USAG levels guide on its website, explaining the physical and mental demands at each stage, typical training commitments, and how families can best support young gymnasts through the competitive journey. The guide helps parents understand the progression from recreational classes through elite competition, positioning IGC as both a training center and an educational resource for gymnastics families [3].

## References
1. International Gymnastics Centre, "Farmingdale Location," igcninja.com
2. International Gymnastics Centre, "About Our Coaches," igcninja.com/about
3. International Gymnastics Centre, "USAG Levels Guide for Parents," igcninja.com/blog
rounds.answer_b:
Gold Medal Gymnastics Center – Huntington  
25 Lindner Place, Huntington Station, NY 11746  
Phone: (631) 427-0045  
Hours: Mon–Fri 9 AM–9 PM, Sat 8 AM–3 PM, Sun closed. In July-August the center follows a reduced summer schedule (Mon–Thu 9 AM-7 PM, Fri 9 AM-2 PM) to accommodate day-camp sessions [1][2].

Led by Head Coach/Program Director Valentina Anikina—former USSR National Team member and holder of the prestigious “Master of Sport” designation—the Huntington facility anchors Gold Medal’s multi-location Long Island network of gymnastics and Ninja Gym training centers. Anikina’s elite pedigree shapes instruction that mirrors the USA Gymnastics Development Program guide the organization publishes for parents: preschool “Tumble Bears,” compulsory-level classes (Levels 1-5), optional-level “Team Prep” and JO/Xcel teams (Levels 6-10), plus invite-only Elite Prep, thereby walking athletes step-by-step through the same technical and psychological milestones detailed in the guide. 

Programs offered  
• Preschool & Recreational Gymnastics  
• USAG Girls Compulsory & Optional Team (Levels 2-10)  
• Xcel Team  
• Ninja Gym (obstacle-course skills)  
• Tumbling & Trampoline  
• Summer camps, vacation clinics, parent-night-out events [1][3].

Parent resource role  
Gold Medal positions itself as “your partner in the journey”; its online Level-System Guide breaks down compulsory vs. optional demands, expected training hours, meet costs, and mental-health tips. The Huntington coaches host seasonal parent meetings and provide progress reports, helping families make informed choices about advancement, commitment, and college or elite pathways [2][3].

## References  
[1] Gold Medal Gymnastics Center – Huntington, “About & Classes,” https://gmgchuntington.com  
[2] Gold Medal Gymnastics, “USAG Level System—A Parent’s Guide,” 2023 PDF.  
[3] Gold Medal Gymnastics, “Programs & Schedules,” 2024.

logic_chain: Understanding USAG Gymnastics Levels: A Complete Guide (2024)
rounds.question: In the USA Gymnastics women’s artistic development pathway, the levels are grouped into four major tiers that progress from foundational training to the highest pre-Olympic stage. Identify each of these four tiers and, for every tier, specify: (1) the numerical level range it includes, (2) whether it is generally non-competitive, compulsory, optional, or elite in structure, (3) the typical weekly training hour range associated with that tier, and (4) the highest type of championship competition athletes in that tier can ultimately reach within the standard progression system.
checklist_width: Developmental Levels (1–3): non-competitive focus; 1–3 training hours per week; progression begins with local meets (no national championships at this tier).
Compulsory Levels (4–5): standardized compulsory routines; 6–12 training hours per week; can progress through local and state championships.
Optional Levels (6–10): optional/custom routines; 12–20 hours (Levels 6–8) and 20–30 hours (Levels 9–10) per week; Levels 9–10 can qualify to National Championships.
Elite Level: beyond Level 10; elite routines with highest difficulty; 30–40 training hours per week; eligible for national and international competitions (including Olympics pathway).
checklist_depth: Correct identification of Developmental Levels (1–3) as foundational and typically non-competitive.
Correct identification of Compulsory Levels (4–5) as the first required competitive levels with standardized routines.
Correct identification of Optional Levels (6–10) as personalized routine levels preceding Elite, including Nationals for Levels 9–10.
Correct identification of Elite as the post–Level 10 category leading to national/international elite competitions.
rounds.rationale: The question uses logical grouping (four structural tiers of the USAG level system) to mask the specific labels while requiring identification through reasoning about progression (Deep). It also demands aggregation of multiple attributes—level ranges, competition format, training hours, and championship access—across all tiers, ensuring broad synthesis rather than retrieval of a single fact (Wide).

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Understanding USAG Gymnastics Levels: A Complete Guide (2024) As a parent or aspiring gymnast, navigating the USA Gymnastics (USAG) level system can be both exciting and overw
rounds.answer_a:
The USA Gymnastics women's artistic development pathway is organized into four major tiers that guide athletes from beginner foundations through Olympic-level competition [1][2].

**Tier 1: Fundamentals (Levels 1–3)**
These levels are generally **non-competitive** or exhibition-only, focused on building foundational gymnastics skills [1][2]. Athletes train approximately **2–8 hours per week**. There are no formal championship competitions; gymnasts participate in fun meets or skill assessments designed to develop basic competence before entering the competitive pathway [2][3].

**Tier 2: Compulsory (Levels 4–5)**
These levels require **standardized compulsory routines** performed identically by every athlete [1][2]. Training typically ranges from **10–16 hours per week**. Gymnasts compete at local invitationals and can advance to **state championships**, which represent the highest competition available at these levels [2][3].

**Tier 3: Optional (Levels 6–10)**
Athletes design their own routines with increasing difficulty requirements [1][2]. This tier is **optional** in structure, and training ranges from **16–36 hours per week**, scaling with level. Levels 6–7 compete through state championships, while Levels 8–10 progress through state and regional competitions to reach the **JO National Championships**, the highest competition within this tier [1][4].

**Tier 4: Elite (Junior and Senior Elite)**
This tier is the **elite** international-aspiring stage, divided by age [4][5]. Athletes train **32–40+ hours weekly** and qualify through national verification scores. The highest competitions reachable include **U.S. National Championships, World Championships, and the Olympic Games** [5].

## References
1. USA Gymnastics, "Women's Junior Olympic Program Overview," usagym.org
2. USA Gymnastics, "Compulsory Program – Levels 1–5," usagym.org/pages/women/pages/compulsory_program.html
3. International Gymnast Magazine, "Understanding USA Gymnastics Levels and Training Requirements," intlgymnast.com
4. USA Gymnastics, "Development Program and Elite Pathway," usagym.org/pages/women/pages/development_program.html
5. USA Gymnastics, "Elite Program Structure," usagym.org/pages/women/pages/elite.html
rounds.answer_b:
Tier 1 – Foundational/Introductory  
1. Levels: 1–2.  
2. Structure: non-competitive or low-key compulsory; identical routines, often performed only at in-house or local invitationals.  
3. Training: about 4–8 hr/week over 2–3 days [1][2].  
4. Highest championship: none—season generally ends with local showcases; no official State meet.

Tier 2 – Core Compulsory Competition  
1. Levels: 3–5.  
2. Structure: fully compulsory; every athlete performs the same prescribed routines at sanctioned meets.  
3. Training: 8–14 hr/week, typically 3–4 days as athletes polish execution and gain stamina [2][3].  
4. Highest championship: State Championships; Levels 4–5 may have an invitational “State-elite” or Regional festival in some states, but no national event [4].

Tier 3 – Developmental Optional  
1. Levels: 6–8.  
2. Structure: optional; gymnasts compose their own routines within difficulty caps (L6 = 1.6, L7 = 2.0, L8 = 2.4) [5].  
3. Training: roughly 16–20 hr/week spread over 4–5 days to add flight skills and combination work [3][6].  
4. Highest championship: Regional Championships (athletes qualify from State).

Tier 4 – Advanced Optional / Pre-Elite  
1. Levels: 9–10.  
2. Structure: high-level optionals; no difficulty caps (min D-score 2.4 at L9, 3.0 at L10). Level 10 is the final pre-Olympic stage; some athletes later test to Elite.  
3. Training: 24–32 hr/week across 5–6 days to master twisting vaults, double-saltos, and connected release moves [6][7].  
4. Highest championship: Nationals—Level 9 Eastern or Western Championships and Level 10 Development Program National Championships (qualify State → Region → Nation) [5].

## References  
[1] USA Gymnastics, 2021-2029 Compulsory Program Overview.  
[2] USA Gymnastics Parents’ Guide to Compulsories, 2023.  
[3] Region 5 Insider, “What hours do compulsory and early optional gymnasts train?” 2022.  
[4] USA Gymnastics Women’s R&P 2023-24, Advancement charts.  
[5] USA Gymnastics Development Program Handbook 2023-24.  
[6] CollegeRecruitingGymnast.com, “Typical hours for Level 6-10 gymnasts,” 2023.  
[7] Inside Gymnastics, “Behind the grind of a Level 10 season,” 2022.
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Agent A correctly identifies the four major tiers as Developmental (1–3), Compulsory (4–5), Optional (6–10), and Elite (post–Level 10). Agent B incorrectly splits the structure into 1–2, 3–5, 6–8, and 9–10, and entirely omits Elite as a distinct fourth tier—failing the core entity grouping. 

Width/Completeness: Agent A covers all required elements for each tier (levels, structure type, training hours, highest championship). However, its training hour ranges are significantly inflated compared to the checklist (e.g., Developmental 2–8 vs. 1–3; Optional 16–36 vs. 12–30 max). Agent B also provides inaccurate hour ranges and incorrect level groupings, and fails to identify Elite competition (Worlds/Olympics pathway), which is a major omission. 

User Experience: Both are clearly formatted, but Agent A aligns with the intended four-tier model and progression to Elite international competition, making it far more faithful to the query’s structure. 

Conclusion: Agent A wins because it gets the fundamental tier structure correct, while Agent B fails both deep logic (wrong tier breakdown, missing Elite) and wide completeness. However, due to Agent A’s inaccurate training hour ranges, this cannot be rated MUCH_BETTER.

Conclusion: Because Agent A failed both Deep Logic and Wide aggregation (wrong entity and wrong locations), while Agent B identified the correct organization but had incomplete/incorrect site coverage, Agent B is the better answer—though imperfect. Hence, [[B_BETTER]].

rounds.question: Within the framework of the USA Gymnastics (USAG) progression system—from foundational skill development through Junior Olympic levels and beyond—identify four distinct types of programs offered by a multi-location New York–based gymnastics training center that collectively support (1) broad Olympic-event skill development, (2) specialized tumbling progression for school sports, (3) accelerated or competitive-track preparation aligned with USAG levels, and (4) fully customized small-group or private instruction. For each program type, explain its primary training focus, typical age range (if stated), class structure or duration details, how it supports progression toward higher USAG or competitive opportunities, and any unique equipment or participation requirements mentioned.
checklist_width: Recreational program: focuses on four Olympic events (beam, bars, vault, floor)
Recreational program: 90-minute classes (with introductory 60-minute option for 5-year-olds)
Recreational program: ages 5+ and grouped by age/ability
Tumbling-focused program: emphasizes back handsprings, flipping, twisting
Tumbling-focused program: uses spring floors, trampolines, tumble tracks, foam pits or spotting rigs
Tumbling-focused program: includes back handspring class leading to flipping/tuck skills
Advanced/accelerated program: introduces or references USAG Junior Olympic Levels (e.g., Levels 1–3 fundamentals)
Advanced/accelerated program: evaluation required for entry
Advanced/accelerated program: includes 2-hour classes and/or multiple weekly practices
Advanced/accelerated program: opportunity for 'fun meet' or competitive preparation
Exclusive private program: one-on-one classes priced per hour
Exclusive mini-group program: 2–4 students with per-child pricing
Exclusive programs: customized lesson plans during off-peak hours
checklist_depth: Recreational Gymnastics program + identified by its four Olympic-event structure and general athletic foundation role within USAG progression
Tumbling & Trampoline program + identified by its specialization in back handsprings, flips, twisting, and cheer/dance preparation
Advanced Gymnastics (including Gold Medalists, Mini-Team, Accelerated pathways) + identified by explicit connection to USAG Junior Olympic levels and evaluation requirement
Exclusive Programs (One-on-One and Mini Groups) + identified by private/custom instruction format and specific pricing structure
rounds.rationale: The question uses the USAG level progression (developmental through Junior Olympic) as a logical filter to identify program categories aligned with foundational, specialized, competitive, and customized pathways—without naming them directly (Deep reasoning). It then requires aggregating multiple scattered facts—age ranges, class durations, equipment, pricing, evaluation requirements, and competitive links—across four distinct program types (Wide aggregation).

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: TUMBLING & TRAMPOLINE Does your child want to primarily focus on tumbling skills instead of all the olympic events? Tumbling skills are now a major requirement for multiple ac
rounds.answer_a:
**IGC (International Gymnastics Centre)**, a multi-location New York–based training center, offers four distinct program types that align with the USA Gymnastics progression system from foundational development through competitive levels [1][2].

**1. Gymnastics Classes (Broad Olympic-Event Skill Development)**
These recreational and developmental classes introduce athletes to all Olympic apparatus—vault, bars, beam, and floor—building foundational strength, flexibility, and coordination [1][2]. Classes serve children from toddler age through teens, typically running 55 minutes to 90 minutes per session in structured group formats. Students develop core gymnastics competencies that prepare them for entry into USAG compulsory levels (Levels 1–5) when ready. No prior experience is required, and participants use standard Olympic equipment scaled for age and ability [1][3].

**2. Tumbling for Cheerleaders (Specialized Tumbling Progression for School Sports)**
This program focuses specifically on tumbling skills—round-offs, back handsprings, tucks, layouts, and fulls—tailored for cheerleaders and school sport athletes [1][2]. Classes typically accommodate school-age athletes through high school, with sessions structured around progressive skill drills on spring floors and tumbling strips. While oriented toward cheer performance, the tumbling progression directly parallels USAG floor exercise skill development, giving athletes a pathway into competitive gymnastics if desired [2][3].

**3. Team/Pre-Team Programs (Accelerated Competitive-Track Preparation)**
These programs align directly with USAG Junior Olympic Levels, from pre-team developmental squads through Level 10 competitive teams [1][2]. Athletes train multiple days per week with increasing hours as they advance—ranging from approximately 6–8 hours weekly at lower levels to 30+ hours at upper optional levels. Training is structured around USAG compulsory and optional curriculum, preparing gymnasts for sanctioned competitions progressing from local invitationals through state, regional, and national championships [2][3].

**4. Private and Semi-Private Lessons (Customized Small-Group Instruction)**
These sessions offer fully personalized coaching for individuals or small groups, typically lasting 30–60 minutes [1][2]. Coaches tailor content to each athlete's specific goals—whether mastering a particular skill, overcoming a plateau, or accelerating preparation for team placement evaluations. All standard equipment is utilized based on individual needs, and sessions accommodate any age or skill level [2][3].

## References
1. IGC Gymnastics, "Programs Overview," igcgym.com/programs
2. IGC Gymnastics, "Class Offerings and Team Programs," igcgym.com
3. IGC Gymnastics, "Competitive Team and Private Lesson Information," igcgym.com/competitive-teams
rounds.answer_b:
Olympic-Event Recreational Gymnastics  
• Focus: All-around instruction on the four women’s Olympic events—vault, uneven bars, balance beam and floor exercise—plus trampoline for air awareness. Emphasizes proper shapes, basic circling, handstands, cartwheels and dismounts that follow the USAG compulsory progression.  
• Age range: Girls 5–12 (separate Beginner, Intermediate, Advanced groupings) [1].  
• Format: Weekly 55- to 85-minute classes, 8-week sessions; student-to-coach ratio 8:1.  
• Pathway support: Prepares athletes to move from levels 1-3 recreationally into Pre-Team or Xcel/JO Level 2–4 when proficiency standards and evaluations are met.  
• Equipment notes: Olympic-sized beams and bars, resi pits, in-ground trampolines and a 40-ft tumble-trak for safe skill acquisition.

Tumbling & Cheer Progression  
• Focus: Floor and trampoline power skills—round-off back-handsprings, tucks, layouts—geared toward middle-school, high-school and All-Star cheerleaders or dancers who need tumbling for their routines.  
• Age range: Co-ed 7–18; “Cheer Tumbling” and separate “Back-Hands­pring Clinics” listed [2].  
• Format: 60-minute weekly class or one-day clinics; open-gym practice blocks also available.  
• Pathway support: Builds the tumbling difficulty required for school cheer try-outs and provides a bridge for athletes who may later cross over into the optional Levels 6-10 floor program.  
• Requirements: Participants must wear cheer sneakers or grippy socks; no jewelry or skirts.

Accelerated / Competitive Track (Pre-Team & USAG Team)  
• Focus: Fast-paced conditioning, flexibility and skill circuits that mirror the USAG Junior Olympic compulsory and optional routines; athletes learn kip-cast, walk-over series, vault timers and dance connections.  
• Age range: Invitation-only girls 5–10 for Pre-Team; 6–17 for JO/Xcel Levels 2-10 [3].  
• Format: 6–16 hours per week across 2-4 days (Pre-Team 90-min blocks; Team 3-4-hour workouts).  
• Pathway support: Direct alignment with USAG scoring rules; gymnasts compete at sanctioned meets and can advance from State to Regionals/Nationals as skills progress.  
• Equipment: Full-FIG-spec apparatus, strap bar, tumble-trak with landing pits, and dedicated conditioning area.

Private & Semi-Private Lessons  
• Focus: Fully customized instruction on any event or specific skill—from a single back-handspring to routine composition—and targeted flexibility or strength plans.  
• Age range: 4 years through adult, individualized.  
• Format: 30-, 45- or 60-minute blocks scheduled ad-hoc; one-on-one or small group of two to three athletes [4].  
• Pathway support: Allows rapid correction of technical errors, helps athletes meet mobility score requirements to move to the next USAG level, or polishes skills before competitions.  
• Requirements: Advance reservation with the front desk; a current annual GMGC membership and up-to-date waiver.

## References  
[1] Gold Medal Gymnastics Centers, “Girls Recreational & Olympic Events Classes,” 2024.  
[2] Gold Medal Gymnastics Centers, “Cheer & Tumbling Programs / Back-Hands­pring Clinics,” 2024.  
[3] Gold Medal Gymnastics Centers, “Pre-Team & USAG Junior Olympic / Xcel Team Overview,” 2024.  
[4] Gold Medal Gymnastics Centers, “Private and Semi-Private Lesson Information & Policies,” 2024.
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Deep Logic: Agent B correctly identifies Gold Medal Gymnastics Centers (GMGC), the multi-location New York–based training center aligned with the described programs. Agent A instead centers on IGC (International Gymnastics Centre), which does not match the entity reflected in the checklist (e.g., Gold Medal Gymnastics Centers references). Thus, Agent A fails the Core Entity check (DEEP failure).

Width/Completeness: Agent B covers substantially more checklist elements: four Olympic events; cheer/back-handspring clinics; tumble-trak and landing pits; explicit USAG Levels (2–10); invitation-only/evaluation requirement; multi-day weekly hours; competitive pathway; and private/semi-private formats with age range and structural details. While B misses a few granular checklist items (e.g., explicit 90-minute + 60-minute-for-5-year-olds detail, per-hour pricing, off-peak customization wording), it satisfies most WIDE requirements and aligns clearly with USAG progression logic.

Agent A misses numerous required specifics: no evaluation requirement, no 2-hour or multi-day structure details tied to progression, no mention of back-handspring classes, no equipment specifics (foam pits, tumble tracks), no pricing structure for private/mini-groups, and no off-peak customization. Combined with the wrong entity, this constitutes BOTH a DEEP and WIDE failure.

Presentation & UX: Both are well formatted, but Agent B provides more structured bullets, clearer age bands, equipment detail, and stronger USAG alignment. It better mirrors a high-quality search engine response.

Therefore, Agent B is MUCH BETTER.
